expressions.expression: I've heard great things about you.
expressions.nuance: 처음 만나는 자리에서 즉시 상대방에게 호감을 전달하는 표현입니다. 단순한 인사치레가 아니라 '나는 당신에 대해 관심을 갖고 있었다'는 메시지를 주어 상대방이 편안함을 느끼게 합니다. 이 표현 뒤에는 반드시 구체적인 내용을 이어가야 공허하지 않습니다 — 누구에게 들었는지, 어떤 프로젝트에 대해 들었는지를 덧붙이면 진정성이 살아납니다.
expressions.dialogue:
You: Hi, I'm Sujin from the product team. I've heard great things about you — specifically how you handled the Seoul launch last quarter.
Alex: Oh, that's kind to hear. It was a challenging few months.
You: I'd love to hear more about it when you have a moment.
Alex: Sure — grab a coffee with me after this session?
expressions.example: Before the conference, he did his homework on every speaker — so when he finally met them, saying 'I've heard great things about you' was genuine.
expressions.practice: I've heard great things about you and the work you've been doing in the APAC region.

expressions.expression: Let's stay in touch.
expressions.nuance: 자리를 마무리할 때 관계를 유지하겠다는 의사를 표현하는 자연스러운 표현입니다. 단, 이 표현은 말로만 끝나면 의례적인 인사가 되기 쉽습니다. 실제로 관계를 이어가려면 바로 그 자리에서 '링크드인 연결할게요', '다음 주에 커피 어때요?' 같은 구체적인 다음 단계를 제안하는 것이 핵심입니다.
expressions.dialogue:
You: It was really great meeting you today. Let's stay in touch — I think there's a lot we could learn from each other.
Maria: Absolutely. Are you on LinkedIn?
You: I am. I'll send you a request tonight.
Maria: Perfect. And ping me if you're ever in Singapore.
expressions.example: At the end of every conference, she'd follow up with at least five people she'd said 'let's stay in touch' to — most people never did.
expressions.practice: Let's stay in touch — I'll send you the article I mentioned and you can share it with your team if it's useful.

expressions.expression: What's keeping you busy these days?
expressions.nuance: 'What do you do?'보다 훨씬 자연스럽고 대화를 열어주는 질문입니다. 직업을 단도직입적으로 묻는 것이 아니라, 상대방이 현재 열정을 쏟고 있는 것을 묻는 방식입니다. 이 질문은 상대방에게 직함이 아닌 이야기를 꺼내게 하고, 그 과정에서 공통 관심사나 협업 기회를 자연스럽게 발견할 수 있습니다.
expressions.dialogue:
You: So what's keeping you busy these days?
Jin: We're deep in a product relaunch — trying to figure out the go-to-market for Southeast Asia.
You: Interesting — we ran a similar campaign in Malaysia last year. Happy to share what worked for us.
Jin: That would be incredibly useful. Can we set up a call?
expressions.example: Instead of leading with 'so, what do you do?' she always asked 'what's keeping you busy these days?' — it made every conversation feel alive.
expressions.practice: I know we only have a few minutes, but I'm curious — what's keeping you busy these days?

expressions.expression: I'd love to pick your brain.
expressions.meaning: 당신의 의견과 경험을 들어보고 싶어요. (비공식적 조언 요청)
expressions.nuance: 'Pick your brain'은 머릿속에서 아이디어를 꺼내 가져간다는 생동감 있는 표현입니다. 공식적인 컨설팅이나 멘토링이 아니라, 가볍게 대화를 통해 인사이트를 얻고 싶을 때 쓰는 표현입니다. 상대방의 전문성을 인정하는 동시에 부담 없는 만남을 제안하는 방식이라 거절하기 어렵게 만드는 힘이 있습니다.
expressions.dialogue:
You: You've been in B2B sales for over ten years — I'd love to pick your brain about enterprise deal cycles.
Sarah: Of course. I always enjoy these conversations.
You: Would a 30-minute virtual coffee work for you next week?
Sarah: Tuesday at 2 PM works. Send me a calendar invite.
expressions.example: Whenever she entered a new industry, she'd identify three people who'd done it before and say 'I'd love to pick your brain.'
expressions.practice: I'd love to pick your brain on how you scaled the customer success team — 20 minutes over coffee?

expressions.expression: It's not what you know, it's who you know.
expressions.meaning: 무엇을 아느냐가 아니라 누구를 아느냐가 중요하다.
expressions.nuance: 네트워킹의 가치를 한 문장으로 압축한 유명한 격언입니다. 능력만큼 인간관계가 기회를 만든다는 현실을 담고 있습니다. 그러나 현대적 해석은 한 걸음 더 나아갑니다: 'It's not just who you know, but who knows you and what they say about you(누가 당신을 알고 뭐라고 말하느냐).' 실력은 기본이고, 그 위에 관계가 기회를 열어준다는 것이 이 표현의 진짜 교훈입니다.
expressions.dialogue:
New Hire: I applied to so many jobs and never heard back. Then my old manager referred me here and I got an interview in two days.
You: That's how it often works. It's not what you know, it's who you know.
New Hire: I used to think that was cynical. Now I see it's just reality.
You: The good news is you can build that network deliberately — it just takes time.
expressions.example: She had all the qualifications but kept hitting walls — until she understood that it's not what you know, it's who you know.
expressions.practice: People say it's not what you know, it's who you know — which is why I invest as much time in relationships as in skills.

master_paragraph.text: Walking into a room full of strangers used to feel like a chore — until I changed my approach. I stopped introducing myself with my job title and started asking, '**What's keeping you busy these days?**' The conversation opens up immediately. When I meet someone remarkable, I say '**I've heard great things about you**' — and I mean it, because I've done my homework. By the end of the evening, I always find one or two people I genuinely want to learn from, and I say '**I'd love to pick your brain**' over coffee. Before we part, '**Let's stay in touch**' isn't a social formality — I follow up within 24 hours. Because in the end, **it's not what you know, it's who you know** — and more importantly, who knows *you*.
master_paragraph.translation: 낯선 사람들로 가득한 방에 들어가는 것은 예전에는 귀찮은 일처럼 느껴졌다 — 내 접근 방식을 바꾸기 전까지는. 나는 직함으로 자신을 소개하는 것을 멈추고 '요즘 어떤 일들로 바쁘세요?'라고 물어보기 시작했다. 대화가 즉시 열린다. 특별한 사람을 만나면 '좋은 말씀 많이 들었어요'라고 말한다 — 그리고 진심으로, 미리 알아봤기 때문이다. 저녁이 끝날 때쯤 나는 항상 진심으로 배우고 싶은 한두 명을 발견하고, '커피 한 잔 하면서 말씀 들을 수 있을까요?'라고 말한다. 헤어지기 전 '계속 연락하고 지내요'는 사교적 형식이 아니다 — 24시간 안에 팔로우업한다. 결국 무엇을 아느냐가 아니라 누구를 아느냐가 중요하기 때문이다 — 더 중요하게는, 누가 당신을 아느냐가.
highlights: What's keeping you busy these days?
I've heard great things about you
I'd love to pick your brain
Let's stay in touch
it's not what you know, it's who you know

expressions.expression: I need to be straight with you.
expressions.nuance: 어려운 말을 꺼내기 전 상대방에게 '지금 하는 말은 진심이고 배려에서 나온다'는 신호를 보내는 표현입니다. '솔직하다(straight)'는 것은 공격이 아니라 정직이라는 뉘앙스를 담고 있어, 방어적인 반응을 줄여줍니다. 이 문장 뒤에는 부드럽지만 명확한 피드백이나 우려 사항이 와야 효과가 있습니다.
expressions.dialogue:
You: I need to be straight with you about something. The quality of the reports has dropped noticeably over the past month.
Ji: I wasn't aware it was that visible.
You: It's not about one report. There's a pattern. I'd rather tell you now before it becomes a bigger problem.
Ji: I appreciate that. Can we talk about what specifically needs to improve?
expressions.example: Before the annual review, she sat down with each team member and said 'I need to be straight with you' — they all said they preferred it to being blindsided.
expressions.practice: I need to be straight with you — I don't think this proposal is ready to go to the client yet.

expressions.expression: I hear you, and...
expressions.nuance: 'I hear you'는 상대방의 감정과 관점을 인정하는 표현입니다. 중요한 것은 'but' 대신 'and'를 쓴다는 점입니다. 'But'은 앞의 말을 무효화하는 느낌을 주지만, 'and'는 두 가지 관점이 동시에 존재할 수 있다는 것을 인정합니다. 어려운 대화에서 상대방의 저항을 줄이는 가장 강력한 언어 도구 중 하나입니다.
expressions.dialogue:
Team Member: This timeline is impossible. I can't deliver that in two weeks.
You: I hear you, and the pressure is real. Let's figure out what's actually achievable in two weeks and what we push to the next sprint.
Team Member: That feels more realistic.
You: Good. Let's prioritize together right now.
expressions.example: Instead of arguing, she said 'I hear you, and here's what I'm seeing from my end' — the conversation shifted from conflict to problem-solving.
expressions.practice: I hear you, and I want to make sure we find a solution that works for both sides.

expressions.expression: Can I share some observations?
expressions.nuance: 직접적인 비판 대신 '관찰(observation)'이라는 중립적 프레임을 사용하는 것이 핵심입니다. '당신은 이렇게 했다'가 아니라 '나는 이것을 관찰했다'는 방식은 사실과 해석을 분리하여 방어적 반응을 줄입니다. 또한 질문 형태로 물어봄으로써 상대방의 허락을 받고 시작하는 것이 심리적 안전감을 만듭니다.
expressions.dialogue:
You: Can I share some observations from this morning's client call?
Colleague: Of course, go ahead.
You: I noticed the client asked twice about the delivery date and we pivoted each time. They might be losing confidence in the timeline.
Colleague: I didn't realize it came across that way. What would you have done differently?
expressions.example: Rather than sending a critical email, she waited until they were alone and asked 'can I share some observations?' — it landed completely differently.
expressions.practice: Before the final presentation, can I share some observations on the structure that might make it stronger?

expressions.expression: This isn't working for me.
expressions.nuance: 불만을 공격 없이 표현하는 가장 깔끔한 방법입니다. '당신이 잘못했다'가 아니라 '이 상황이 나에게 효과적이지 않다'는 자신의 경험을 중심으로 말하는 방식입니다. 경계를 설정하거나 업무 방식에 대한 재협상을 요청할 때, 관계를 파괴하지 않으면서도 분명한 입장을 전달할 수 있습니다.
expressions.dialogue:
You: I want to flag something. The current feedback process isn't working for me — getting comments on a document two hours before the deadline doesn't give me enough time to incorporate them meaningfully.
Manager: That's fair. What would work better?
You: A 24-hour review window before any major deliverable. Would that be feasible?
Manager: Let's make that the new standard.
expressions.example: Instead of silently resenting the situation for months, she finally said 'this isn't working for me' — and they fixed it in one conversation.
expressions.practice: The late-night Slack messages aren't working for me — I'd like to set some boundaries around response time after 8 PM.

expressions.expression: Let's separate the issue from the person.
expressions.nuance: 갈등이 개인 감정으로 번지는 것을 막는 강력한 프레임입니다. 사람을 공격하거나 방어하는 것이 아니라, '우리가 함께 해결해야 할 문제'를 중심에 놓는 방식입니다. 이렇게 하면 자존심을 건드리지 않고도 핵심을 다룰 수 있습니다. 특히 팀 내 갈등을 중재하거나 책임 소재가 불명확한 상황에서 매우 효과적입니다.
expressions.dialogue:
Team A: Team B keeps missing their deadlines and it's blocking our work.
You: Let's separate the issue from the person here. The problem is a dependency in our workflow that isn't working. Let's focus on fixing that structure rather than pointing fingers.
Team B: That's fair. The handoff process is genuinely unclear.
You: Good. Let's redesign that first.
expressions.example: The mediation went well because the facilitator kept bringing everyone back to 'let's separate the issue from the person.'
expressions.practice: I know tensions are high, but if we can separate the issue from the person, I think we'll solve this much faster.

master_paragraph.text: The hardest conversation I ever had started with three words: '**I need to be straight with you.**' A senior colleague's work had been consistently below the standard the team needed, and everyone knew it but no one said it. I opened by asking, '**Can I share some observations?**' — framing it as what I'd seen, not what they'd done wrong. When they pushed back, I said '**I hear you, and** the pressure you're under is real.' That 'and' kept the door open. I was clear about what **wasn't working for me** and what needed to change. Finally, I asked us to **separate the issue from the person** — because this wasn't about them as a human being; it was about a pattern we needed to fix together. They thanked me afterward. They said it was the most useful conversation they'd had all year.
master_paragraph.translation: 내가 했던 가장 어려운 대화는 세 단어로 시작됐다: '솔직하게 말씀드려야 할 것 같아요.' 한 선임 동료의 업무가 지속적으로 팀이 필요로 하는 기준에 미치지 못했고, 모두가 알고 있었지만 아무도 말하지 않았다. 나는 '제가 느낀 점을 말씀드려도 될까요?'라고 물으며 시작했다 — 그들이 무엇을 잘못했는지가 아니라 내가 무엇을 관찰했는지로 프레이밍했다. 그들이 반박했을 때 나는 말했다: '말씀하시는 거 이해해요, 그리고 지금 받고 계신 압박감은 실제로 있는 것이에요.' 그 '그리고'가 문을 열어두었다. 나는 나에게 맞지 않는 것이 무엇인지, 무엇이 바뀌어야 하는지 명확히 했다. 마지막으로, 나는 문제와 사람을 분리해서 이야기하자고 제안했다 — 이것은 한 인간으로서의 그들에 관한 것이 아니라, 우리가 함께 고쳐야 할 패턴에 관한 것이었기 때문이다. 그들은 나중에 감사하다고 했다. 그해 가장 유용한 대화였다고.
highlights: I need to be straight with you
Can I share some observations?
I hear you, and
wasn't working for me
separate the issue from the person

expressions.expression: I want to recognize what you did.
expressions.nuance: 성과에 대한 인정은 보너스나 승진만큼이나 강력한 동기부여 도구입니다. 특히 공개적인 자리나 즉각적인 인정은 '내 노력이 보이고 있다'는 심리적 안전감을 줍니다. 막연한 칭찬('잘했어')보다 구체적인 행동을 명시하는 것이 훨씬 효과적입니다 — '당신이 클라이언트와의 위기 상황에서 보여준 침착함을 인정하고 싶어요'처럼.
expressions.dialogue:
You: Before we move on, I want to recognize what you did this week. You rewrote the entire API documentation in three days to meet the launch deadline.
Developer: It was a lot of nights, honestly.
You: And it showed. The client team specifically mentioned how clear it was. That kind of work doesn't go unnoticed.
Developer: That really means a lot to hear.
expressions.example: She made it a habit to open every team meeting by recognizing what someone had done — morale improved within a month.
expressions.practice: I want to recognize what you did on the Johnson account — handling that objection under pressure was exactly the kind of leadership we need.

expressions.expression: I trust your judgment on this.
expressions.nuance: 자율성은 내재적 동기부여의 핵심입니다. '내가 결정해줄게'가 아니라 '당신이 결정해도 돼'라는 말은 권한 위임의 가장 강력한 표현입니다. 이 말을 들은 사람은 단순한 실행자가 아닌 의사결정자로서의 책임감을 갖게 됩니다. 단, 이 표현은 실제로 그 사람의 역량을 신뢰할 때만 써야 합니다 — 겉으로만 위임하고 나중에 개입하면 오히려 신뢰를 깨뜨립니다.
expressions.dialogue:
You: I trust your judgment on this vendor. You've done the due diligence and know the requirements better than anyone.
Lead: Are you sure? It's a big contract.
You: That's exactly why I want you to make the call. You've earned it.
Lead: Okay. I'll go with Option B and document my reasoning.
expressions.example: He didn't micromanage — he'd say 'I trust your judgment on this' and mean it, which made the team perform at a higher level.
expressions.practice: I trust your judgment on the hiring decision — you've interviewed all three candidates and you know what the team needs.

expressions.expression: What do you need from me to make this happen?
expressions.meaning: 이것을 실현하기 위해 제가 어떻게 도와드릴까요?
expressions.nuance: 리더십의 방향을 '내가 지시한다'에서 '내가 지원한다'로 바꾸는 표현입니다. 이 질문은 팀원에게 주체성을 돌려주고, 관리자는 장애물을 제거하는 역할로 자신을 재정의합니다. 서번트 리더십(servant leadership)의 핵심 언어이기도 합니다. 이 질문을 진심으로 묻고 실제로 행동으로 옮기는 리더를 팀원은 신뢰하고 따릅니다.
expressions.dialogue:
Team Member: I know what needs to be done, but there are three blockers I can't resolve on my own.
You: Tell me what they are. What do you need from me to make this happen?
Team Member: I need access to the Q3 data, a sign-off from legal, and a 30-minute conversation with the CTO.
You: I can get you all three by Friday. Let's remove those blockers.
expressions.example: The project turned around when the director stopped asking for status updates and started asking 'what do you need from me to make this happen?'
expressions.practice: You've got the vision and the skills to pull this off. What do you need from me to make this happen?

expressions.expression: You've really grown in this role.
expressions.nuance: 성과(what someone did)를 인정하는 것과 성장(how someone has developed)을 인정하는 것은 다릅니다. 성장에 대한 인정은 더 깊은 수준의 동기부여를 만듭니다 — '내가 더 나은 사람이 됐다'는 것을 누군가 알아봤다는 느낌이기 때문입니다. 성과 리뷰나 1:1 미팅에서 단순한 결과 평가를 넘어 이 표현을 쓰면 훨씬 기억에 남는 대화가 됩니다.
expressions.dialogue:
You: I want to say something that's not about deliverables. You've really grown in this role over the past year.
Yuna: In what way?
You: A year ago you'd ask for my approval on every decision. Now you own the whole process and only escalate when you genuinely need to. That's a big shift.
Yuna: I hadn't thought about it that way. Thank you.
expressions.example: The best feedback she ever received wasn't about a specific project — it was her manager saying 'you've really grown in this role,' and naming exactly how.
expressions.practice: You've really grown in this role — the confidence you bring to client conversations now is completely different from six months ago.

expressions.expression: I've got your back.
expressions.nuance: 등 뒤(back)를 지켜준다는 이미지에서 나온 표현입니다. 단순한 응원이 아니라, '당신이 어떤 위험을 감수하든 내가 방패가 되어줄 것'이라는 구체적인 보호의 약속입니다. 팀원이 새로운 시도를 두려워하거나, 실패가 두려워서 도전을 망설일 때 이 말 한 마디가 심리적 안전감을 만들어 행동을 가능하게 합니다.
expressions.dialogue:
Team Member: I want to try a completely different approach for this pitch, but I'm worried if it doesn't land, it'll reflect badly on the team.
You: Try it. I've got your back. If it doesn't work, we'll learn from it together. If it does, that's a new playbook for all of us.
Team Member: Okay. I'll go for it.
You: That's what I wanted to hear.
expressions.example: The team started taking creative risks the moment their new manager said 'I've got your back' and proved it when things went sideways.
expressions.practice: Present your recommendation honestly, even if it's unpopular. I've got your back in that room.

master_paragraph.text: The best leaders I've worked for had one thing in common: they made you feel seen. When something went well, they'd say, '**I want to recognize what you did**' — and name the specific action, not just the outcome. When a decision needed to be made, they'd say, '**I trust your judgment on this**' and actually step back. When things got hard, they asked, '**What do you need from me to make this happen?**' — then removed the blockers. And over time, they noticed not just what you delivered but who you'd become: '**You've really grown in this role.**' When something felt risky, they looked you in the eye and said, '**I've got your back.**' That's not management. That's the reason people stay.
master_paragraph.translation: 내가 함께 일한 최고의 리더들에게는 한 가지 공통점이 있었다: 그들은 당신을 보이게 만들었다. 좋은 일이 있을 때 그들은 '당신이 한 일을 인정하고 싶어요'라고 말했다 — 그리고 결과가 아닌 구체적인 행동을 언급했다. 결정이 필요할 때 그들은 '이 부분은 당신의 판단을 믿어요'라고 말하고 실제로 물러났다. 힘들어질 때 그들은 '이것을 실현하기 위해 제가 어떻게 도와드릴까요?'라고 물었다 — 그리고 장애물을 제거했다. 그리고 시간이 지나면서 그들은 당신이 무엇을 해냈는지만이 아니라 당신이 누가 됐는지를 알아차렸다: '이 역할에서 정말 많이 성장했어요.' 무언가가 위험하게 느껴질 때 그들은 눈을 맞추고 말했다: '내가 당신 편이에요.' 그것은 관리가 아니다. 그것이 사람들이 남아 있는 이유다.
highlights: I want to recognize what you did
I trust your judgment on this
What do you need from me to make this happen?
You've really grown in this role
I've got your back

english_theme_packs.theme_title: 면접과 커리어 개발 대화를 할 때

expressions.expression: Tell me about a time when...
expressions.nuance: 이것은 가장 보편적인 행동 면접(Behavioral Interview) 질문의 시작 패턴입니다. STAR 기법(Situation·Task·Action·Result)으로 답하는 것이 기본입니다. 이 패턴을 아는 것은 면접관과 면접자 모두에게 중요합니다 — 면접관으로서는 구체적인 과거 행동을 묻는 표준 언어이고, 면접자로서는 추상적인 자기 선전이 아닌 이야기 구조로 답해야 한다는 신호입니다.
expressions.dialogue:
Interviewer: Tell me about a time when you had to manage a project with conflicting stakeholder priorities.
You: Sure. Last year, our product launch had conflicting input from sales and engineering. I set up a structured decision framework that mapped each request to our core business goal.
Interviewer: What was the outcome?
You: We launched on time, and both teams later said the process was clearer than anything they'd experienced before.
expressions.example: She prepared 12 STAR stories before the interview so that any 'tell me about a time when' question had a ready answer.
expressions.practice: Tell me about a time when you turned a failure into a learning opportunity for your team.

expressions.expression: What does success look like in this role?
expressions.nuance: 면접에서 지원자가 던지는 가장 강력한 질문 중 하나입니다. 단순히 직무 설명을 넘어 '90일 후, 1년 후에 어떤 상태여야 하는가'를 묻는 것입니다. 이 질문은 두 가지를 동시에 보여줍니다: 결과 지향적 사고방식과, 기대치를 사전에 정렬하려는 프로다운 태도. 면접관 입장에서도 이 질문을 받으면 '이 사람은 진지하다'는 인상을 받습니다.
expressions.dialogue:
You: Before we finish, I have a question. What does success look like in this role at the 6-month mark?
Hiring Manager: That's a great question. By six months, I'd want you to have built rapport with the key stakeholders and delivered your first independent project.
You: And at 12 months?
Hiring Manager: Ideally, you'd be mentoring someone more junior. That's how I know you've fully landed.
expressions.example: Her final question in every interview was always the same: 'What does success look like in this role?' — it always changed the energy in the room.
expressions.practice: What does success look like in this role after the first 90 days, from your perspective?

expressions.expression: I'm looking for my next challenge.
expressions.nuance: 'Why are you leaving your current job?'에 대한 가장 세련된 답변 프레임입니다. 현재 직장의 나쁜 점을 말하는 대신, 자신의 성장 방향에 집중하는 방식입니다. 이직을 도망이 아닌 전진으로 프레이밍한다는 점에서 면접관에게 긍정적인 인상을 줍니다. '도전'이라는 단어는 자신이 안정보다 성장을 추구한다는 메시지를 내포합니다.
expressions.dialogue:
Interviewer: Why are you leaving your current role?
You: I've learned a great deal in the past three years and I'm proud of what I built there. But I'm looking for my next challenge — specifically the chance to work on problems at a larger scale.
Interviewer: What kind of scale are you thinking?
You: Global product rollouts with cross-functional teams. That's exactly why this role caught my attention.
expressions.example: Every time she changed jobs, she said the same thing: 'I'm looking for my next challenge' — and she always meant it.
expressions.practice: After five years in the same vertical, I feel ready. I'm looking for my next challenge — one that pushes me into new markets.

expressions.expression: Where do you see yourself in five years?
expressions.meaning: 5년 후 자신의 모습은 어떻게 그리고 있나요?
expressions.nuance: 가장 고전적인 면접 질문이지만, 지금도 자주 쓰입니다. 정답은 '이 회사에서 성장하겠다'도, '정직하게 모르겠다'도 아닙니다. 핵심은 자신의 성장 방향을 명확히 알고 있으면서, 그 방향이 지원 회사의 기회와 연결될 수 있음을 보여주는 것입니다. 너무 구체적으로 '5년 후 이 자리의 매니저가 되겠다'고 하면 역효과가 날 수 있습니다 — 방향성과 열정을 보여주되, 유연함도 함께 전달해야 합니다.
expressions.dialogue:
Interviewer: Where do you see yourself in five years?
You: I want to have built deep expertise in product strategy and led at least one product from zero to significant scale. I'm less focused on a specific title and more on the type of impact I want to have.
Interviewer: That aligns well with what we're building here.
You: Which is a big part of why I applied.
expressions.example: 'Where do you see yourself in five years?' used to trip him up — until he realized the question was about direction, not destination.
expressions.practice: Where do you see yourself in five years, and how does this role fit into that path?

expressions.expression: I'd like to negotiate the offer.
expressions.nuance: 연봉 협상은 많은 사람들이 가장 어렵게 느끼는 직장 대화입니다. 하지만 협상을 요청하는 것 자체는 전혀 무례한 것이 아닙니다 — 오히려 자신의 가치를 아는 전문가의 자세입니다. 핵심은 공격적이지 않게, 근거를 가지고 협상 테이블을 여는 것입니다. 시장 데이터, 현재 연봉 수준, 자신의 경험을 근거로 삼고, 숫자보다 먼저 '협의하고 싶다'는 의사를 표현하는 것이 자연스러운 시작입니다.
expressions.dialogue:
Recruiter: So, are you ready to move forward with the offer?
You: I'm genuinely excited about this role. I'd like to negotiate the offer if that's possible — specifically around base salary.
Recruiter: Of course. What did you have in mind?
You: Based on my research and current compensation, I was hoping we could get to 85 million. Is there flexibility there?
expressions.example: She was told 'the offer is firm' twice before — but both times, when she said 'I'd like to negotiate the offer,' they found flexibility.
expressions.practice: Before I sign, I'd like to negotiate the offer — the role is a great fit but I want to make sure we're aligned on compensation.

master_paragraph.text: Walking into an interview prepared means more than knowing your resume. It means knowing the language. When they ask '**Tell me about a time when** you failed,' don't panic — that's a STAR question, and you've prepared three stories. Before the interview ends, you ask: '**What does success look like in this role** at six months?' It tells them you think in outcomes, not tasks. If they ask why you're leaving, you say '**I'm looking for my next challenge**' — not that you're running from something. When they ask '**Where do you see yourself in five years?**', answer with direction, not a job title. And when the offer comes, remember: getting the offer means they want you. That's the moment to say, '**I'd like to negotiate the offer.**' Professionals negotiate. They hired you because you're one.
master_paragraph.translation: 면접에 준비된 상태로 들어간다는 것은 이력서를 아는 것 이상입니다. 언어를 아는 것을 의미합니다. '실패했을 때에 대해 말해줘요'라고 물으면 당황하지 마세요 — 그것은 STAR 질문이고, 당신은 세 가지 이야기를 준비했습니다. 면접이 끝나기 전 물어보세요: '6개월 시점에 이 역할에서 성공이 어떤 모습인가요?' 그것은 당신이 할 일이 아닌 결과로 생각한다는 것을 보여줍니다. 왜 떠나냐고 물으면, '다음 도전을 찾고 있어요'라고 말하세요 — 뭔가에서 도망치는 것이 아니라. '5년 후 어디에 있고 싶나요?'라고 물으면, 직함이 아닌 방향성으로 답하세요. 그리고 제안이 왔을 때 기억하세요: 제안을 받았다는 것은 그들이 당신을 원한다는 뜻입니다. 그것이 '제안 조건에 대해 협의하고 싶어요'라고 말하는 순간입니다. 전문가는 협상합니다. 그들이 당신을 채용한 이유도 그 때문입니다.
highlights: Tell me about a time when
What does success look like in this role
I'm looking for my next challenge
Where do you see yourself in five years?
I'd like to negotiate the offer

expressions.expression: Here's the problem we're solving.
expressions.nuance: 좋은 피치는 솔루션이 아니라 문제에서 시작합니다. 청중이 '이 문제가 실제로 존재한다'는 것을 인정해야만 솔루션에 귀를 기울이기 때문입니다. 문제를 먼저 선명하게 정의하는 것은 청중과의 공감대를 만들고, 이후 제안하는 솔루션의 필요성을 논리적으로 뒷받침합니다. 피치의 첫 번째 슬라이드나 첫 번째 문장을 이 프레임으로 시작하는 것이 가장 효과적입니다.
expressions.dialogue:
You: Before I show you the solution, let me start with here's the problem we're solving. Right now, it takes our sales team 4 hours to generate a single proposal. That's 20 hours a week per person just on documentation.
Investor: That does sound like a bottleneck.
You: Exactly. And when we surveyed 200 sales managers, 87% said it was their biggest time drain.
Investor: Okay. Now I'm listening. What's the solution?
expressions.example: The pitch didn't start with the product — it started with 'here's the problem we're solving,' and the room leaned forward.
expressions.practice: Let me frame this properly: here's the problem we're solving, and it's bigger than most people realize.

expressions.expression: The insight behind this is...
expressions.nuance: 'Insight'는 누구나 볼 수 있는 데이터가 아니라, 그 데이터를 깊이 들여다봤을 때만 보이는 비자명한 진실을 의미합니다. 피치에서 인사이트를 명확히 하는 것은 '우리는 남들이 보지 못한 것을 봤다'는 경쟁 우위의 선언입니다. 강력한 인사이트는 '아, 그렇구나' 하는 반응을 만들어냅니다 — 그 반응이 나오면 피치는 이미 절반은 성공한 것입니다.
expressions.dialogue:
You: The insight behind this is that people don't abandon products because they stop needing them — they abandon them because the product stopped surprising them.
Partner: That's interesting. Where did that come from?
You: Exit survey analysis across 40,000 churned users. The top reason wasn't 'too expensive' or 'found a better alternative' — it was 'became boring.'
Partner: That fundamentally reframes the problem.
expressions.example: He didn't present a product. He presented an insight — 'the insight behind this is that trust is lost in seconds and built in months' — and the room went quiet.
expressions.practice: The insight behind this is something our competitors are consistently missing — and that gap is our entire opportunity.

expressions.expression: Imagine a world where...
expressions.nuance: 추상적인 솔루션을 구체적인 이미지로 전환하는 스토리텔링 기법입니다. 사람들은 기능 목록이 아니라 이야기와 이미지에 감동합니다. '우리 제품은 A, B, C를 합니다'보다 '이런 세상을 상상해보세요, 그것이 우리가 만드는 것입니다'가 훨씬 기억에 남습니다. TED 강연과 실리콘밸리 피치 모두 이 프레임을 자주 사용하는 이유입니다.
expressions.dialogue:
You: Imagine a world where a small business owner in Seoul can access the same financial tools that Goldman Sachs uses — in under five minutes, on a phone.
Audience: [engaged silence]
You: That's not a fantasy. We've built it. And 12,000 users are already using it.
Audience: How does the pricing work?
expressions.example: She opened the pitch with 'imagine a world where every child has a personal tutor' — by the end, three investors had already leaned over to their partners.
expressions.practice: Imagine a world where reporting takes 10 minutes instead of 10 hours. That's the specific problem we built this for.

expressions.expression: We've already validated this with...
expressions.nuance: 피치에서 가장 흔한 실패 원인은 아이디어에서 멈추는 것입니다. '검증됐다'는 증거는 아이디어를 현실로 격상시킵니다. 특히 초기 단계의 스타트업이나 내부 제안에서 파일럿 결과, 사용자 인터뷰 데이터, POC(개념 증명) 결과가 있으면 반드시 포함해야 합니다. '우리가 생각하기에...'보다 '우리가 이미 검증한 바에 의하면...'이 천 배 설득력이 있습니다.
expressions.dialogue:
Skeptic: This is an interesting idea, but do you have any evidence it actually works?
You: We've already validated this with a 60-day pilot across three enterprise clients. Average time savings per user: 6 hours per week.
Skeptic: And the clients are still using it?
You: All three renewed and two have asked to expand the rollout company-wide.
expressions.example: The pitch turned when she said 'we've already validated this with 200 beta users' — suddenly it wasn't a concept, it was a business.
expressions.practice: We're not asking you to take a leap of faith. We've already validated this with a $2M live contract in the Korean market.

expressions.expression: What we're asking for is...
expressions.nuance: 피치의 가장 중요한 순간은 'ask(요청)'입니다. 많은 사람들이 훌륭한 발표를 하고도 마지막에 '혹시 관심 있으시면...'처럼 흐지부지하게 끝냅니다. 명확하고 구체적인 ask는 청중에게 다음 단계를 안내하고, '이 사람은 자신이 원하는 게 뭔지 알고 있다'는 신뢰를 줍니다. 금액, 기간, 그리고 그것으로 무엇을 달성할지를 함께 명시하는 것이 완성된 ask입니다.
expressions.dialogue:
You: So, what we're asking for is a $500K seed investment over 18 months. That gets us to product-market fit and our first 100 paying customers.
Investor: And what's the milestone at 18 months?
You: $1M ARR and a Series A ready data room.
Investor: That's a clear ask. Let's schedule a deeper dive.
expressions.example: After 20 minutes of a brilliant pitch, he finally said 'what we're asking for is simple: three months of pilot funding.' The room relaxed — they finally knew what to say yes to.
expressions.practice: What we're asking for is simple: three months and a team of two to prove this works in your market.

master_paragraph.text: A great pitch tells a story in five moves. First, you anchor with the problem: '**Here's the problem we're solving**' — stated so clearly that the audience nods. Then you deliver the surprise: '**The insight behind this is**...' — the non-obvious truth that makes them lean in. You paint the vision: '**Imagine a world where**...' — not a feature list, but a feeling. Then you bring it back to earth: '**We've already validated this with**...' — data that transforms a dream into a plan. And finally, you close with clarity: '**What we're asking for is**...' — a specific, confident ask that makes it easy to say yes. Most pitches fail not because the idea is bad, but because they skip one of these five moves.
master_paragraph.translation: 훌륭한 피치는 다섯 가지 움직임으로 이야기를 전합니다. 첫째, 문제로 시작합니다: '우리가 해결하려는 문제는 이겁니다' — 청중이 고개를 끄덕일 만큼 명확하게 설명합니다. 그 다음 놀라움을 전합니다: '이 아이디어의 핵심 통찰은...' — 그들을 앞으로 기울게 만드는 비자명한 진실. 비전을 그립니다: '만약 ~한 세상을 상상해보세요' — 기능 목록이 아니라 감정으로. 그런 다음 현실로 돌아옵니다: '이미 ~을 통해 검증했어요' — 꿈을 계획으로 바꾸는 데이터. 그리고 마지막으로 명확하게 마무리합니다: '저희가 요청드리는 것은...' — '예스'라고 말하기 쉽게 만드는 구체적이고 확신 있는 요청. 대부분의 피치가 실패하는 것은 아이디어가 나빠서가 아니라, 이 다섯 가지 움직임 중 하나를 건너뛰기 때문입니다.
highlights: Here's the problem we're solving
The insight behind this is
Imagine a world where
We've already validated this with
What we're asking for is

english_expressions.meaning: 분위기를 파악하다.
english_expressions.example: Before jumping into the agenda, she paused to read the room — the team was clearly exhausted from the previous session.
english_expressions.dialogue:
A: Why did you change your presentation style halfway through?
B: I had to read the room. They weren't responding to data — they needed a story.
A: Smart. The energy shifted completely after that.
B: Always watch the audience more than your own slides.

english_expressions.meaning: 시작하다, 물꼬를 트다.
english_expressions.example: We've been discussing this for two months — it's time to get the ball rolling on the actual work.
english_expressions.dialogue:
A: Who's going to take the first step?
B: I'll get the ball rolling. I'll send out the project charter by end of today.
A: Perfect. That's all we needed — someone to start.
B: Once the first domino falls, the rest usually follow.

english_expressions.meaning: 잠깐 연락하다, 진행 상황을 확인하다.
english_expressions.example: Let's touch base on Friday to see where things stand before the client presentation.
english_expressions.dialogue:
A: I haven't heard from the vendor in a week.
B: Let me touch base with them today and see what's holding things up.
A: Appreciate it. Even a brief status would help.
B: I'll have an update for you before noon.

english_expressions.meaning: 반론을 제기하는 역할 (의도적으로 반대 입장을 취하다)
english_expressions.example: Let me play devil's advocate — what if our assumptions about user behavior are completely wrong?
english_expressions.dialogue:
A: Everyone seems to agree the new pricing model is the right move.
B: Can I play devil's advocate for a moment?
A: Please do.
B: What if our premium users are price-sensitive in ways we haven't measured yet? We might be solving the wrong problem.

english_expressions.meaning: 쉽게 얻을 수 있는 성과, 먼저 해결하기 쉬운 것
english_expressions.example: Before we tackle the big structural changes, let's go after the low-hanging fruit and fix the onboarding flow.
english_expressions.dialogue:
A: We need quick wins to restore stakeholder confidence after the Q2 miss.
B: The low-hanging fruit is the checkout abandonment rate — it's 67% and fixable in two weeks.
A: Do it. We need something to show by month end.
B: Consider it done.

english_expressions.meaning: (업무적) 여유 시간, 처리 용량
english_expressions.example: I don't have the bandwidth to take on another project this sprint — something has to come off my plate first.
english_expressions.dialogue:
A: Can you join the website redesign working group?
B: I'd love to, but I genuinely don't have the bandwidth right now. I'm carrying three critical deliverables this month.
A: Understood. When would be realistic?
B: Check back in four weeks — things should open up after the launch.

english_expressions.meaning: 세부 사항에 너무 깊이 빠져 있다, 큰 그림을 못 보고 있다
english_expressions.example: We've been in the weeds on the technical specs for an hour — let's step back and realign on the business goal.
english_expressions.dialogue:
A: I think we're getting in the weeds here. The exec team doesn't need to know how the algorithm works — they need to know what problem it solves.
B: You're right. I keep defaulting to technical details.
A: Start with the outcome, then offer the details only if they ask.
B: Good reminder. Let me reframe the whole thing.

english_expressions.meaning: 직접적인 이해관계가 있다, 자기 자신도 위험을 감수하다
english_expressions.example: The vendor was more cooperative once they had skin in the game — we tied 20% of their fee to delivery outcomes.
english_expressions.dialogue:
A: How do we make sure the consulting firm is actually invested in the outcome, not just billing hours?
B: Give them skin in the game. Structure the contract so part of their fee depends on measurable results.
A: They'll push back on that.
B: That's fine. The ones who push back don't believe in their own work.

english_expressions.meaning: 인지하고 있도록 하다, 주의를 기울이게 하다.
english_expressions.example: I don't need a decision now, but I wanted to put the budget overrun on your radar before the board meeting.
english_expressions.dialogue:
A: I just wanted to put something on your radar — the API migration is taking longer than expected.
B: Are we at risk of missing the Q3 deadline?
A: Not yet, but we might be in two weeks if we don't add a resource.
B: Thanks for flagging it early. Let's revisit Thursday.

english_expressions.meaning: 틀에서 벗어나 창의적으로 생각하다.
english_expressions.example: The conventional approach isn't working — this is a moment that calls for thinking outside the box.
english_expressions.dialogue:
A: Every solution we've tried follows the same playbook and it keeps failing.
B: We need to think outside the box. What if we approached this as a distribution problem rather than a product problem?
A: That's a completely different frame.
B: Exactly. The box was the wrong shape.

english_expressions.meaning: 상사나 윗사람과의 관계를 능동적으로 관리하다
english_expressions.example: Learning to manage up is one of the most underrated career skills — understanding what your boss needs before they ask.
english_expressions.dialogue:
A: My manager keeps asking for things at the last minute and it's stressing me out.
B: Have you tried managing up? Proactively sharing your weekly priorities on Monday can set expectations before they become surprises.
A: I never thought of it that way. So I'm shaping their expectations?
B: Exactly. It makes you more predictable and it puts you in control.

english_expressions.meaning: 결정하지 못하고 있다, 중간 입장이다
english_expressions.example: She was on the fence about accepting the offer until they added the remote work option.
english_expressions.dialogue:
A: Where is the client on the proposal?
B: Still on the fence. They like the approach but are nervous about the price.
A: What would tip them?
B: A case study from a similar-sized company and maybe a payment split. That usually does it.

english_expressions.meaning: 기준을 높이다
english_expressions.example: This year's campaign raised the bar for what we expect from every future launch.
english_expressions.dialogue:
A: The new design standards are going to slow us down initially.
B: That's the point. We need to raise the bar on quality before we scale. Speed without quality just scales the problems.
A: Fair. Short-term pain, long-term gain.
B: Exactly. And once the team internalises the new standard, speed will come back.

english_expressions.meaning: 빠르게 시작하다, 신속히 세팅하다
english_expressions.example: We need to spin up a task force this week to address the security vulnerability.
english_expressions.dialogue:
A: The client needs a proof of concept by the end of the month.
B: That's tight but doable. I can spin up a small team — two engineers and a designer.
A: What do you need from me?
B: Executive sponsorship so I can pull people off their current sprints.

english_expressions.meaning: 빠르게 움직이고 실수를 두려워하지 마라. (스타트업 문화의 핵심 표현)
english_expressions.example: The early-stage startup culture was 'move fast and break things' — iteration speed was everything.
english_expressions.dialogue:
A: We keep waiting for the perfect version. When do we actually ship?
B: Move fast and break things. Ship the 80% version, learn from real users, fix what breaks. You'll never hit 100% in the lab.
A: What if something breaks in production?
B: Fix it faster than a competitor would have launched.

english_expressions.meaning: 더 깊이 파고들다, 세부 사항을 탐구하다
english_expressions.example: The headline number looks good, but we need to drill down into the regional breakdown.
english_expressions.dialogue:
A: Revenue is up 18% overall.
B: Let's drill down — which segments are driving that? Because I suspect it's masking weakness in the core product.
A: You're right. When we break it down, new product lines are up 60% and core is actually flat.
B: That's a completely different story.

english_expressions.meaning: 그건 제 권한 밖의 일이에요. 제가 결정할 수 없어요.
english_expressions.example: I can give you my recommendation, but final approval on that budget is above my pay grade.
english_expressions.dialogue:
A: Can you approve the additional $200K directly?
B: That's above my pay grade — anything over $100K needs CFO sign-off. But I can put together the business case and walk it through myself.
A: That would help a lot. When could you do that?
B: By Thursday.

english_expressions.meaning: 즉시 본격적으로 시작하다, 처음부터 속력을 내다
english_expressions.example: She hit the ground running in her new role — by week two she'd already identified three quick wins.
english_expressions.dialogue:
A: Our new head of growth starts Monday. What's the onboarding plan?
B: We want her to hit the ground running, so we've set up 30-minute intros with every key stakeholder in her first week.
A: And her first deliverable?
B: A 30-60-90 day plan by the end of week two.

english_expressions.meaning: 모두가 알지만 아무도 말하지 않는 불편한 문제
english_expressions.example: We can talk about channel strategy all day, but the elephant in the room is that our core product hasn't improved in two years.
english_expressions.dialogue:
A: Can we talk about the elephant in the room? Revenue has been flat for three quarters and we keep discussing tactics instead of strategy.
B: I'm glad you said it. I think everyone's been waiting for someone to raise it.
A: So what's the real problem?
B: Honestly? We're solving for the wrong customer.

english_expressions.meaning: 그건 타당한 지적이에요.
english_expressions.example: That's a fair point — I hadn't considered the impact on the downstream teams.
english_expressions.dialogue:
A: If we reduce the review time to 24 hours, quality control might suffer.
B: That's a fair point. What if we add a lightweight checklist to maintain the quality bar without the full review cycle?
A: That could work.
B: Let's pilot it on the next three projects and see.

english_expressions.meaning: 불가능하거나 비효율적으로 너무 많은 것을 한 번에 하려 하다
english_expressions.example: Trying to fix every broken process at once is boiling the ocean — pick the one with the highest ROI.
english_expressions.dialogue:
A: We want to overhaul the entire customer journey — from awareness to retention.
B: That's boiling the ocean. You'll spend six months and change nothing. Pick the one stage with the most friction and fix that.
A: Probably the onboarding.
B: Then go deep there. Master one thing before you expand.

english_expressions.expression: A rising tide lifts all boats.
english_expressions.meaning: 전체가 잘 되면 모두에게 좋다. 환경이 좋아지면 모두 혜택을 받는다.
english_expressions.example: Instead of competing internally for budget, we reminded the leadership team that a rising tide lifts all boats.
english_expressions.dialogue:
A: Why should we share our customer research with the other product teams?
B: A rising tide lifts all boats. If the other teams improve their products, it makes the whole platform stronger — and that brings more customers to our product too.
A: That's a more collaborative way to think about it.
B: In a platform business, internal competition is almost always self-defeating.

english_expressions.meaning: 이 부분에 대해서 우리는 같은 입장이에요.
english_expressions.example: Before the client meeting, confirm that we're aligned on this — there can't be conflicting messages in the room.
english_expressions.dialogue:
A: So everyone agrees the priority for Q4 is retention, not acquisition?
B: We're aligned on this. All three teams have confirmed retention is the north star.
A: Good. Let's make sure that message is consistent in every external conversation.
B: Agreed. I'll put it in writing in the meeting notes.

english_expressions.meaning: 이것에 대해 좀 더 자세히 풀어주세요.
english_expressions.example: Before we react, can we unpack this a bit more — specifically what the customer actually said versus what we interpreted?
english_expressions.dialogue:
A: The survey results show that customers find us 'confusing.'
B: Can we unpack that a bit more? Confusing how — the product, the pricing, the messaging?
A: From the open-ended responses, it seems to be the pricing structure.
B: Okay, that's a much more actionable insight. That we can fix.

english_expressions.expression: The proof is in the pudding.
english_expressions.meaning: 결과가 모든 것을 증명한다. 말이 아닌 결과로 보여줘야 한다.
english_expressions.example: We can debate the strategy all we want, but the proof is in the pudding — let's look at the pilot results.
english_expressions.dialogue:
A: The team is skeptical about the new framework. They think it's just another process that adds overhead.
B: The proof is in the pudding. Run it for one sprint and let the velocity numbers speak.
A: And if it doesn't improve things?
B: Then we have our answer. But I've seen it work in four other teams.
